annotations.comment: better title?

paragraphs.body: In the article "47 New Orleans Inspired Recipes," Penelope Denby lists forty-seven recipes for New Orleans-inspired foods. She includes desserts like beignets, banana foster and king cake, seafood from crawfish, blackened catfish, half a dozen shrimp recipes, and staples of New Orleans such as red beans and rice, Jambalaya, and black-eyed peas. First, Denby gives you an overview of the recipe. She gives a picture of the food and how long it will take you to make it for a serving size of 6 people. Also, Denby gives a concise, fun, and unique description of what the food is and what it tastes like. Next, she gives a link to the recipe, with a detailed direction for every ingredient you need and an in-depth description of the step-by-step process you will need to complete the recipe. Finally, Denby gives the nutritional facts for the desired food you are making. New Orleans has some of the best food in the world. We have amazing restaurants and food trucks, and on a random Saturday night, at 2 am, you could go to a gas station and eat a hot plate of some of the best fried chicken and red beans and rice you have ever had. But to me, what makes the culture of food in New Orleans so great is that everyone has someone in their family who sets the standard for a meal. The best compliment you can hear if you make someone food in New Orleans is, "This is the second best dish I had only to that family member's meal." In my family, the person who sets the standard is my mama with her filet mignon. When I come home, step out of the car, and smell the air, and it is my mama's cooking, I know I am in for a good night. When I left home in New Orleans for college in Pittsburgh, I quickly realized my meals would not be the same. Cynthia Ozick, a short story writer and novelist, asserts, "We often take for granted the very things that most deserve our gratitude." Ozick's point is that the thing most important to us is that we often do not recognize them. I was unaware of how special my mama's meals were until I was in the dorm room with no one to cook for me.

paragraphs.body: After a few weeks of eating food at Pitt dining halls and carrying around Tony's seasoning everywhere, I had enough. I went to Target and got an air fryer, a hotplate, and a crockpot. I have become obsessed with learning how to cook. I would go on the internet and look up recipes and test different seasoning combinations, but when I had a question or needed advice, I would call my mama. One of my favorite meals is when my mama makes a filet mignon, so when I got one at the grocery store, my mom walked me through the way she cooks it step by step. I got better at cooking with each meal, and every time I would cook, I tasted a little bit of home.

paragraphs.body: Making a good meal takes significant effort, and I did not have the energy to do that daily. I put a little less care into the food, and I tasted the difference. Having flavorful food was not a good enough reason for me to take the time to create it. The only time I would have a passion for making a meal was when my friend would ask for some food. A chef and cookbook author, Thomas Keller, writes, "A recipe has no soul. As the cook, you must bring the soul to the recipe." In other words, Keller believes that if you are not enthusiastic about cooking, nothing you do can make the food taste good. The reason the food is so perfect in New Orleans is because it is made with soul. I looked forward to lunch every day at my high school. I walked into the cafeteria, and the scent of the slow-cooked beans, with hints of garlic and onion, Italian Sausage, and Tony's seasoning surrounded the room. I would be in line waiting with my mouth watering and make it to the front while the cafeteria lady, smiling from ear to ear, would hand your hotplate and say, "Enjoy, baby." I would walk as fast as possible, eagerly anticipating that first bite. I'd sit down, pour a little more Tony's, mix it around the beans, and finally, I got to dig my fork into the beans, and the wait was over. No one would bring lunch from home; you see everyone with a hot plate enjoying their meal as we got the much-needed break from class, laughing about whatever happened that day. I sometimes looked over to where the cafeteria lady sat after she had served everyone, and no one was happier than her after he had served 500 students another amazing meal. It can be the cafeteria lady or a gourmet chef in a 5-star restaurant. What makes the meal worthwhile is the fulfillment they get when someone enjoys their meal. There is no better representation of this than my mama. She would drop my brother and me off, go to work for 8 hours, bring us to practices, and then go back home. There had to be nights when she was exhausted and did not feel like cooking, but every night, she ensured my brother and I had a meal cooked with her soul. I always chuckle when people say that their mama or family member makes the best food because there can only be one person who makes the best food. But when someone says that, they are not lying or embellishing their family members. They really do mean that because they see the sacrifice that goes into those meals, and that makes the food taste better. So when someone asks what my favorite food is? There is only one answer: my mama filet mignon.

I would be in line waiting with my mouth watering and make it to the front while the cafeteria lady, smiling from ear to ear, would hand your hotplate and say, "Enjoy, baby."

paragraphs.body: New Orleans has set my expectation of good food at a remarkably high bar, but I have come to understand that it is not the food I miss but the culture behind it. I miss walking into the cafeteria and the smell encircling it. I miss walking into a restaurant and chef who would be ninety years old and have pictures of them serving every president back to John F. Kennedy. I miss going home after a long day from school and practice and sitting there with my brother waiting for my mom to serve the food she put everything into. My mama showed and taught me what love is through the sacrifice she made every day, making sure My brother and I were fed. I have learned that I am not obsessed with learning how to cook, but with sharing that love my mama taught me through food with the people I care about.
